revision.text:
'''金刺 盛澄'''（かなさし もりずみ）とは、[[平安時代]]後期の[[諏訪神社]]下宮の神官。
[[源義仲]]と親密な関係にあったため、義仲の討伐後、[[源頼朝]]によって捕縛され、[[梶原景時]]に預けられた。頼朝は盛澄を処刑しようとしていたが、盛澄が卓越した弓の名手であったことから、景時は盛澄の命を奪うのを惜しみ、頼朝に説得を重ねた末、せめて盛澄の弓の技量を見てから死罪にして欲しい、と請願する。盛澄の頼朝の基に参上し、[[流鏑馬]]を披露した。この時頼朝は盛澄が騎乗する馬としてわざと暴れ馬を与えた上、盛澄が指定された八つの的を射抜くと、射抜いた的の破片、さらに的を立てかけた串を射抜くよう命じたが、盛澄は見事に全て射抜いたため、赦免された。この時、景時が同じく捕縛された義仲の郎党達にも寛恕を施して欲しい、と頼朝に願い出て、その郎党達もまた助命されたという。この顛末は『諏訪大明神絵詞』で描写されている。
{{DEFAULTSORT:かなさし もりすみ}} [[Category:諏訪氏]]

'''金刺 盛澄'''（かなさし もりずみ）とは、[[平安時代]]後期の[[諏訪神社]]下宮の神官。
[[源義仲]]と親密な関係にあったため、義仲の討伐後、[[源頼朝]]によって捕縛され、[[梶原景時]]に預けられた。頼朝は盛澄を処刑しようとしていたが、盛澄が卓越した弓の名手であったことから、景時は盛澄の命を奪うのを惜しみ、頼朝に説得を重ねた末、せめて盛澄の弓の技量を見てから死罪にして欲しい、と請願する。盛澄の頼朝の基に参上し、[[流鏑馬]]を披露した。この時頼朝は盛澄が騎乗する馬としてわざと暴れ馬を与えた上、盛澄が指定された八つの的を射抜くと、射抜いた的の破片、さらに的を立てかけた串を射抜くよう難題を押し付けてきたが、盛澄は見事に全て射抜いたため、赦免された。この時、景時が同じく捕縛された義仲の郎党達にも寛恕を施して欲しい、と頼朝に願い出て、その郎党達もまた助命されたという。この顛末は『諏訪大明神絵詞』で描写されている。
{{DEFAULTSORT:かなさし もりすみ}} [[Category:諏訪氏]]

'''金刺 盛澄'''（かなさし もりずみ）とは、[[平安時代]]後期の[[諏訪神社]]下宮の神官。弟に[[手塚光盛]]がいる。
[[源義仲]]と親密な関係にあったため、義仲の討伐後、[[源頼朝]]によって捕縛され、[[梶原景時]]に預けられた。頼朝は盛澄を処刑しようとしていたが、盛澄が卓越した弓の名手であったことから、景時は盛澄の命を奪うのを惜しみ、頼朝に説得を重ねた末、せめて盛澄の弓の技量を見てから死罪にして欲しい、と請願する。盛澄は頼朝の基に参上し、[[流鏑馬]]を披露した。この時頼朝は盛澄が騎乗する馬としてわざと暴れ馬を与えた上、盛澄が指定された八つの的を射抜くと、射抜いた的の破片、さらに的を立てかけた串を射抜くよう難題を押し付けてきたが、盛澄は見事に全て射抜いたため、赦免された。この時、景時が同じく捕縛された義仲の郎党達にも寛恕を施して欲しい、と頼朝に願い出て、その郎党達もまた助命されたという。この顛末は『諏訪大明神絵詞』で描写されている。
{{DEFAULTSORT:かなさし もりすみ}} [[Category:諏訪氏]]

'''金刺 盛澄'''（かなさし もりずみ）とは、[[平安時代]]後期の[[諏訪大社]]下宮の[[神官]]。[[武士]]。'''諏訪盛澄'''とも呼ばれる<ref>[http://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84 諏訪盛澄【すわ・もりずみ】]、[[コトバンク]]、2009年11月12日閲覧 </ref>。弟に[[手塚光盛]]がいる。
[[源義仲]]と親密な関係にあったため、義仲の討伐後、[[源頼朝]]によって捕縛され、[[梶原景時]]に預けられた。頼朝は盛澄を処刑しようとしていたが、盛澄が卓越した弓の名手であったことから、景時は盛澄の命を奪うのを惜しみ、頼朝に説得を重ねた末、せめて盛澄の弓の技量を見てから死罪にして欲しい、と請願する。盛澄は頼朝の基に参上し、[[流鏑馬]]を披露した。この時頼朝は盛澄が騎乗する馬としてわざと暴れ馬を与えた上、盛澄が指定された八つの的を射抜くと、射抜いた的の破片、さらに的を立てかけた串を射抜くよう難題を押し付けてきたが、盛澄は見事に全て射抜いたため、赦免された。この時、景時が同じく捕縛された義仲の郎党達にも寛恕を施して欲しい、と頼朝に願い出て、その郎党達もまた助命されたという。この顛末は『諏訪大明神絵詞』で描写されている。

'''金刺 盛澄'''（かなさし もりずみ）とは、[[平安時代]]後期の[[諏訪大社]]下宮の[[神官]]・[[武士]]。'''諏訪盛澄'''とも呼ばれる<ref>[http://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84 諏訪盛澄【すわ・もりずみ】]、[[コトバンク]]、2009年11月12日閲覧 </ref>。弟に[[手塚光盛]]がいる。

'''金刺 盛澄'''（かなさし もりずみ）とは、[[平安時代]]後期の[[諏訪大社]]下宮の[[神官]]・[[武士]]。'''諏訪盛澄'''とも呼ばれる<ref>[http://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84 諏訪盛澄【すわ・もりずみ】]、[[コトバンク]]、2009年11月12日閲覧 </ref>。弟に[[手塚光盛]]がいる。
[[源義仲]]と親密な関係にあったため、義仲の討伐後、[[源頼朝]]によって捕縛され、[[梶原景時]]に預けられた。頼朝は盛澄を処刑しようとしていたが、盛澄が卓越した弓の名手であったことから、景時は盛澄の命を奪うのを惜しみ、頼朝に説得を重ねた末、せめて盛澄の弓の技量を見てから死罪にして欲しい、と請願する。盛澄は頼朝の基に参上し、[[流鏑馬]]を披露した。この時頼朝は盛澄が騎乗する馬としてわざと暴れ馬を与えた上、盛澄が指定された八つの的を射抜くと、射抜いた的の破片、さらに的を立てかけた串を射抜くよう難題を押し付けてきたが、盛澄は見事に全て射抜いたため、赦免された。この時、景時が同じく捕縛された義仲の郎党達にも寛恕を施して欲しい、と頼朝に願い出て、その郎党達もまた助命されたという。この顛末は『諏訪大明神絵詞』で描写されている。
その後盛澄は御家人となり、流鏑馬や的始の儀式で活躍した。史料では1203年までの活動が見られる。[[下諏訪町]]には、盛澄が恩人である景時を偲び建立した梶原塚がある。

[[ファイル:Suwa Morizumi.jpg|thumb|250px|諏訪盛澄（金刺盛澄）／[[江戸時代]]『[[前賢故実]]』より。画：[[菊池容斎]]]] '''金刺 盛澄'''（かなさし の もりずみ、生没年未詳）とは、[[平安時代]]後期の[[諏訪大社]]下宮の[[神官]]・[[武士]]。'''諏訪盛澄'''とも呼ばれる。弟に[[手塚光盛]]がいる。
== 生涯 == [[源義仲]]と親密な関係にあったため、義仲の討伐後、[[源頼朝]]によって捕縛され、[[梶原景時]]に預けられた。頼朝は盛澄を[[処刑]]しようとしていたが、盛澄が卓越した[[弓術|弓]]の名手であったことから、景時は盛澄の命を奪うのを惜しみ、頼朝に説得を重ねた末、せめて盛澄の弓の技量を見てから死罪にして欲しい、と請願する。盛澄は頼朝の基に参上し、[[流鏑馬]]を披露した。この時頼朝は盛澄が騎乗する[[ウマ|馬]]としてわざと暴れ馬を与えた上、盛澄が指定された八つの的を射抜くと、射抜いた的の破片、さらに的を立てかけた串を射抜くよう難題を押し付けてきたが、盛澄は見事に全て射抜いたため、赦免された。この時、景時が同じく捕縛された義仲の郎党達にも寛恕を施して欲しい、と頼朝に願い出て、その郎党達もまた助命されたという。この顛末は『諏訪大明神絵詞』で描写されている。
その後盛澄は[[御家人]]となり、流鏑馬や的始の儀式で活躍した。史料では[[1203年]]までの活動が見られる。[[下諏訪町]]には、盛澄が恩人である景時を偲び建立した梶原[[塚]]がある。
== 参考文献 == *信濃毎日新聞社「長野県百科事典」 *[[朝倉書店]]「日本中世史事典」
== 関連項目 == *[[諏訪神党]] *[[諏訪氏]]

== 生涯 == [[源義仲]]と親密な関係にあったため、義仲の討伐後、[[源頼朝]]によって捕縛され、[[梶原景時]]に預けられた。頼朝は盛澄を[[処刑]]しようとしていたが、盛澄が卓越した[[弓術|弓]]の名手であったことから、景時は盛澄の命を奪うのを惜しみ、頼朝に説得を重ねた末、せめて盛澄の弓の技量を見てから死罪にして欲しい、と請願する。盛澄は頼朝の基に参上し、[[流鏑馬]]を披露した。この時頼朝は盛澄が騎乗する[[ウマ|馬]]としてわざと暴れ馬を与えた上、盛澄が指定された八つの的を射抜くと、射抜いた的の破片、さらに的を立てかけた串を射抜くよう難題を押し付けてきたが、盛澄は見事に全て射抜いたため、赦免された。この時、景時が同じく捕縛された義仲の郎党達にも寛恕を施して欲しい、と頼朝に願い出て、その郎党達もまた助命されたという。この顛末は『諏訪大明神絵詞』で描写されている。
その後は[[御家人]]となり、流鏑馬や的始の儀式で活躍した。史料では[[建仁]]3年（[[1203年]]）までの活動が見られる。[[下諏訪町]]には盛澄が恩人の景時を偲び建立した梶原[[塚]]がある。

[[ファイル:Suwa Morizumi.jpg|thumb|250px|諏訪盛澄（金刺盛澄）／[[江戸時代]]『[[前賢故実]]』より。[[菊池容斎]]画]] '''金刺 盛澄'''（かなさし の もりずみ、生没年未詳）は、[[平安時代]]後期の[[諏訪大社]]下宮の[[神官]]・[[武士]]。'''諏訪盛澄'''とも呼ばれる。弟に[[手塚光盛]]がいる。
== 生涯 == 当初は[[治承・寿永の乱]]での[[源義仲]]の挙兵に従ったが、御射山神事のため弟の光盛を留め置いて帰国した。[[伊勢平氏|平家]]の家人でもあったことから、義仲の討伐後、[[源頼朝]]によって捕縛され、[[梶原景時]]に預けられた。頼朝は盛澄を[[処刑]]しようとしていたが、盛澄が[[藤原秀郷]]流[[弓術]]を継承する名手であったことから、景時は盛澄の命を奪うのを惜しみ、頼朝に説得を重ねた末、せめて盛澄の弓の技量を見てから死罪にして欲しい、と請願する。

これらの顛末は『[[諏方大明神画詞|諏訪大明神絵詞]]』でも描写されている。
その後は[[御家人]]となり、流鏑馬や的始の儀式で活躍した。史料では[[建仁]]3年（[[1203年]]）までの活動が見られる。[[下諏訪町]]には盛澄が恩人の景時を偲び建立した梶原[[塚]]がある。

[[ファイル:Suwa Morizumi.jpg|thumb|250px|諏訪盛澄（金刺盛澄）／[[江戸時代]]『[[前賢故実]]』より。[[菊池容斎]]画]] '''金刺 盛澄'''（かなさし の もりずみ、生没年未詳）は、[[平安時代]]後期の[[諏訪大社]]下宮の[[神官]]・[[武士]]。'''諏訪盛澄'''とも呼ばれる。弟に[[手塚光盛]]がいる。
== 生涯 == 当初は[[治承・寿永の乱]]での[[源義仲]]の挙兵に従ったが、御射山神事のため弟の光盛を留め置いて帰国した。[[伊勢平氏|平家]]の家人でもあったことから、義仲の討伐後、[[源頼朝]]によって捕縛され、[[梶原景時]]に預けられた。頼朝は盛澄を[[処刑]]しようとしていたが、盛澄が[[藤原秀郷]]流[[弓術]]を継承する名手であったことから、景時は盛澄の命を奪うのを惜しみ、頼朝に説得を重ねた末、せめて盛澄の弓の技量を見てから死罪にして欲しい、と請願する。
盛澄は頼朝の基に参上し、[[鶴岡八幡宮]][[放生会]]で[[流鏑馬]]を披露した。この時頼朝は盛澄が騎乗する[[ウマ|馬]]としてわざと暴れ馬を与えた上、盛澄が指定された八つの的を射抜くと、射抜いた的の破片、さらに的を立てかけた串を射抜くよう難題を押し付けてきたが、盛澄は見事に全て射抜いたため、赦免された。この時、景時が同じく捕縛された義仲の郎党達にも寛恕を施して欲しい、と頼朝に願い出て、その郎党達もまた助命されたという（「[[吾妻鏡]]」文治3年8月15日条）。
これらの顛末は『[[諏方大明神画詞|諏訪大明神絵詞]]』でも描写されている。
その後は[[御家人]]となり、流鏑馬や的始の儀式で活躍した。史料では[[建仁]]3年（[[1203年]]）までの活動が見られる。[[下諏訪町]]には盛澄が恩人の景時を偲び建立した梶原[[塚]]がある。

[[ファイル:Suwa Morizumi.jpg|thumb|250px|諏訪盛澄（金刺盛澄）／[[江戸時代]]『[[前賢故実]]』より。[[菊池容斎]]画]] '''金刺 盛澄'''（かなさし の もりずみ、生没年未詳）は、[[平安時代]]後期の[[諏訪大社]]下宮の[[神官]]・[[武士]]。'''諏訪盛澄'''とも呼ばれる。弟に[[手塚光盛]]がいる。
== 生涯 == 当初は[[治承・寿永の乱]]での[[源義仲]]の挙兵に従ったが、御射山神事のため弟の光盛を留め置いて帰国した。[[伊勢平氏|平家]]の家人として長年在京していたことから（『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[文治]]3年8月15日条）、義仲の討伐後、[[源頼朝]]によって捕縛され、[[梶原景時]]に預けられた。頼朝は盛澄を[[処刑]]しようとしていたが、盛澄が[[藤原秀郷]]流[[弓術]]を継承する名手であったことから、景時は盛澄の命を奪うのを惜しみ、頼朝に説得を重ねた末、せめて盛澄の弓の技量を見てから死罪にして欲しい、と請願する。
盛澄は頼朝の基に参上し、[[鶴岡八幡宮]][[放生会]]で[[流鏑馬]]を披露した。この時頼朝は盛澄が騎乗する[[ウマ|馬]]としてわざと暴れ馬を与えた上、盛澄が指定された八つの的を射抜くと、射抜いた的の破片、さらに的を立てかけた串を射抜くよう難題を押し付けてきたが、盛澄は見事に全て射抜いたため、赦免された。この時、景時が同じく捕縛された義仲の郎党達にも寛恕を施して欲しい、と頼朝に願い出て、その郎党達もまた助命され、[[御家人]]に列した（『[[吾妻鏡]]』文治3年8月15日条）。これらの顛末は『[[諏方大明神画詞|諏訪大明神絵詞]]』でも描写されている。
[[建久]]4年（1193年）3月、頼朝が[[下野国]][[那須]]、[[上野国]]三原で行った[[狩倉]]にも随伴し、以後は幕府の流鏑馬や的始の儀式で活躍した。同年9月、[[北条義時]]の元服前の「[[矢開き|矢口餅]]の儀式」では三の口を賜っている（『[[吾妻鏡]]』建久3年9月11日条）。史料では[[建仁]]3年（[[1203年]]）までの活動が見られる。[[下諏訪町]]には盛澄が恩人の景時を偲び建立した梶原[[塚]]がある。

== 生涯 == 当初は[[治承・寿永の乱]]での[[源義仲]]の挙兵に従ったが、御射山神事のため弟の光盛を留め置いて帰国した。[[伊勢平氏|平家]]の家人として長年在京していたことから（『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[文治]]3年8月15日条）、義仲の討伐後、[[源頼朝]]によって捕縛され、[[梶原景時]]に預けられた。頼朝は盛澄を[[処刑]]しようとしていたが、盛澄が[[藤原秀郷]]流[[弓術]]を継承する名手であったことから、景時は盛澄の命を奪うのを惜しみ、頼朝に説得を重ねた末、せめて盛澄の弓の技量を見てから死罪にして欲しい、と請願する。
盛澄は頼朝の基に参上し、[[鶴岡八幡宮]]での[[放生会]]で[[流鏑馬]]を披露した。この時頼朝は盛澄が騎乗する[[ウマ|馬]]としてわざと暴れ馬を与えた上、盛澄が指定された八つの的を射抜くと、射抜いた的の破片、さらに的を立てかけた串を射抜くよう難題を押し付けてきたが、盛澄は見事に全て射抜いたため、赦免された。この時、景時が同じく捕縛された義仲の郎党達にも寛恕を施して欲しい、と頼朝に願い出て、その郎党達もまた助命され、[[御家人]]に列した（『[[吾妻鏡]]』文治3年8月15日条）。これらの顛末は『[[諏方大明神画詞|諏訪大明神絵詞]]』でも描写されている。
[[建久]]4年（1193年）3月、頼朝が[[下野国]][[那須]]、[[上野国]]三原で行った[[狩倉]]にも随伴し、以後は幕府の流鏑馬や的始の儀式で活躍した。同年9月、[[北条泰時]]の元服前の「[[矢開き|矢口餅]]の儀式」では三の口を賜っている（『[[吾妻鏡]]』建久4年9月11日条）。史料では[[建仁]]3年（[[1203年]]）までの活動が見られる。[[下諏訪町]]には盛澄が恩人の景時を偲び建立した梶原[[塚]]がある。

== 関連項目 == *[[諏訪神党]] *[[諏訪氏]] *[[山吹御前]] == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かなさし の もりすみ}} [[Category:金刺氏|もりすみ]] [[Category:諏訪氏|もりすみ]] [[Category:平安時代の武士]] [[Category:平安時代の神職]] [[Category:鎌倉幕府御家人]] [[Category:鎌倉時代の神職]] [[Category:源義仲]] [[Category:治承・寿永の乱の人物]] [[Category:12世紀生]] [[Category:13世紀没]]

[[ファイル:Suwa Morizumi.jpg|thumb|250px|諏訪盛澄（金刺盛澄）／[[江戸時代]]『[[前賢故実]]』より。[[菊池容斎]]画]] '''金刺 盛澄'''（かなさし の もりずみ、生没年未詳）は、[[平安時代]]後期の[[諏訪大社]][[諏訪大社#下社|下社]]の[[神官]]（[[諏訪大社#神職|大祝]]）・[[武士]]<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm なんでも諏訪百科『金刺盛澄』] - [[諏訪市博物館]]</ref>。'''諏訪盛澄'''とも呼ばれる<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm なんでも諏訪百科『諏訪盛澄』諏訪市博物館]</ref>。弟に[[手塚光盛]]がいる。
== 生涯 == 当初は[[治承・寿永の乱]]での[[源義仲]]の挙兵に従ったが、御射山神事のため弟の光盛を留め置いて帰国した。[[伊勢平氏|平家]]の家人として長年在京していたことから（『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[文治]]3年8月15日条）、義仲の討伐後、[[源頼朝]]によって捕縛され、[[梶原景時]]に預けられた。頼朝は盛澄を[[処刑]]しようとしていたが、盛澄が[[藤原秀郷]]流[[弓術]]を継承する名手であったことから、景時は盛澄の命を奪うのを惜しみ、頼朝に説得を重ねた末、せめて盛澄の弓の技量を見てから死罪にして欲しい、と請願する。
盛澄は頼朝の基に参上し、[[鶴岡八幡宮]]での[[放生会]]で[[流鏑馬]]を披露した。この時頼朝は盛澄が騎乗する[[ウマ|馬]]としてわざと暴れ馬を与えた上、盛澄が指定された八つの的を射抜くと、射抜いた的の破片、さらに的を立てかけた串を射抜くよう難題を押し付けてきたが、盛澄は見事に全て射抜いたため、赦免された。この時、景時が同じく捕縛された義仲の郎党達にも寛恕を施して欲しい、と頼朝に願い出て、その郎党達もまた助命され、[[御家人]]に列した<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 『諏訪盛澄』] - [[コトバンク]]</ref>（『[[吾妻鏡]]』文治3年8月15日条）。これらの顛末は『[[諏方大明神画詞|諏訪大明神絵詞]]』でも描写されている。
[[建久]]4年（1193年）3月、頼朝が[[下野国]][[那須]]、[[上野国]]三原で行った[[狩倉]]にも随伴し、以後は幕府の流鏑馬や的始の儀式で活躍した。同年9月、[[北条泰時]]の元服前の「[[矢開き|矢口餅]]の儀式」では三の口を賜っている（『[[吾妻鏡]]』建久4年9月11日条）。史料では[[建仁]]3年（[[1203年]]）までの活動が見られる。[[下諏訪町]]には盛澄が恩人の景時を偲び建立した梶原[[塚]]がある。
== 画像集 == <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|梶原塚（下諏訪町）（[[長野県]][[諏訪郡]]下諏訪町木の下） 梶原塚全景.jpg|梶原塚全景(右へ諏訪大社下宮[[諏訪大社#秋宮|秋宮]]) </gallery>
== 参考文献 == *『長野県百科事典』[[信濃毎日新聞]]社 *『日本中世史事典』[[朝倉書店]]

{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>

{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>
## References * ''Nagano Encyclopedia'' (長野県百科事典), published by {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞}}. * ''Dictionary of Medieval Japanese History'' (日本中世史事典), published by {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}.
## See also * {{ill|Suwa Shinbō|ja|諏訪神党}} * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏}} * {{ill|Yamabuki Gozen|ja|山吹御前}} * {{ill|Orihiromitsu|ja|折井宏光}} - Historical painter who depicted Kanasa Mori-sumi.<ref>[http://suwagunshihaku.org/article/56094157.html 諏訪湖博物館特別展: "Drawing Kanasa Mori-sumi ~ Orihiromitsu Exhibition" (2012)]</ref>

revision.text:
{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>
## References * ''Nagano Encyclopedia'' (長野県百科事典), published by {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞}}. * ''Dictionary of Medieval Japanese History'' (日本中世史事典), published by {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}.
## See also * {{ill|Suwa Shinbō|ja|諏訪神党}} * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏}} * {{ill|Yamabuki Gozen|ja|山吹御前}} * {{ill|Orihiromitsu|ja|折井宏光}} - Historical painter who depicted Kanasa Mori-sumi.<ref>[http://suwagunshihaku.org/article/56094157.html 諏訪湖博物館特別展: "Drawing Kanasa Mori-sumi ~ Orihiromitsu Exhibition" (2012)]</ref>
## Notes

{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>

In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.

{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>

Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.

## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>

revision.text:
{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>
## References * ''Nagano Encyclopedia'' (長野県百科事典), published by {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞}}. * ''Dictionary of Medieval Japanese History'' (日本中世史事典), published by {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}.
## See also * {{ill|Suwa Shinbō|ja|諏訪神党}} * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏}} * {{ill|Yamabuki Gozen|ja|山吹御前}} * {{ill|Orihiromitsu|ja|折井宏光}} - Historical painter who depicted Kanasa Mori-sumi.<ref>[http://suwagunshihaku.org/article/56094157.html 諏訪湖博物館特別展: "Drawing Kanasa Mori-sumi ~ Orihiromitsu Exhibition" (2012)]</ref>

{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>
## References * ''Nagano Encyclopedia'' (長野県百科事典), published by {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞}}. * ''Dictionary of Medieval Japanese History'' (日本中世史事典), published by {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}.
## See also * {{ill|Suwa Shinbō|ja|諏訪神党}} * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏}} * {{ill|Yamabuki Gozen|ja|山吹御前}} * {{ill|Orihiromitsu|ja|折井宏光}} - Historical painter who depicted Kanasa Mori-sumi.<ref>[http://suwagunshihaku.org/article/56094157.html 諏訪湖博物館特別展: "Drawing Kanasa Mori-sumi ~ Orihiromitsu Exhibition" (2012)]</ref>

{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>
## References * ''Nagano Encyclopedia'' (長野県百科事典), published by {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞}}. * ''Dictionary of Medieval Japanese History'' (日本中世史事典), published by {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}.
## See also * {{ill|Suwa Shinbō|ja|諏訪神党}} * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏}} * {{ill|Yamabuki Gozen|ja|山吹御前}} * {{ill|Orihiromitsu|ja|折井宏光}} - Historical painter who depicted Kanasa Mori-sumi.<ref>[http://suwagunshihaku.org/article/56094157.html 諏訪湖博物館特別展: "Drawing Kanasa Mori-sumi ~ Orihiromitsu Exhibition" (2012)]</ref>

{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>

## See also * {{ill|Suwa Shinbō|ja|諏訪神党}} * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏}} * {{ill|Yamabuki Gozen|ja|山吹御前}} * {{ill|Orihiromitsu|ja|折井宏光}} - Historical painter who depicted Kanasa Mori-sumi.<ref>[http://suwagunshihaku.org/article/56094157.html 諏訪湖博物館特別展: "Drawing Kanasa Mori-sumi ~ Orihiromitsu Exhibition" (2012)]</ref>

{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>

{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>
## References * ''Nagano Encyclopedia'' (長野県百科事典), published by {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞}}. * ''Dictionary of Medieval Japanese History'' (日本中世史事典), published by {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}.
## See also * {{ill|Suwa Shinbō|ja|諏訪神党}} * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏}} * {{ill|Yamabuki Gozen|ja|山吹御前}} * {{ill|Orihiromitsu|ja|折井宏光}} - Historical painter who depicted Kanasa Mori-sumi.<ref>[http://suwagunshihaku.org/article/56094157.html 諏訪湖博物館特別展: "Drawing Kanasa Mori-sumi ~ Orihiromitsu Exhibition" (2012)]</ref>

{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>

{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>

'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>
## References * ''Nagano Encyclopedia'' (長野県百科事典), published by {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞}}. * ''Dictionary of Medieval Japanese History'' (日本中世史事典), published by {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}.
## See also * {{ill|Suwa Shinbō|ja|諏訪神党}} * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏}} * {{ill|Yamabuki Gozen|ja|山吹御前}} * {{ill|Orihiromitsu|ja|折井宏光}} - Historical painter who depicted Kanasa Mori-sumi.<ref>[http://suwagunshihaku.org/article/56094157.html 諏訪湖博物館特別展: "Drawing Kanasa Mori-sumi ~ Orihiromitsu Exhibition" (2012)]</ref>

revision.text:
{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>
## References * ''Nagano Encyclopedia'' (長野県百科事典), published by {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞}}. * ''Dictionary of Medieval Japanese History'' (日本中世史事典), published by {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}.
## See also * {{ill|Suwa Shinbō|ja|諏訪神党}} * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏}} * {{ill|Yamabuki Gozen|ja|山吹御前}} * {{ill|Orihiromitsu|ja|折井宏光}} - Historical painter who depicted Kanasa Mori-sumi.<ref>[http://suwagunshihaku.org/article/56094157.html 諏訪湖博物館特別展: "Drawing Kanasa Mori-sumi ~ Orihiromitsu Exhibition" (2012)]</ref>

'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.

{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>

By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.

{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>

{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.

'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.

revision.text:
{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>
## References * ''Nagano Encyclopedia'' (長野県百科事典), published by {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞}}. * ''Dictionary of Medieval Japanese History'' (日本中世史事典), published by {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店}}.
## See also * {{ill|Suwa Shinbō|ja|諏訪神党}} * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan}} * {{ill|Yamabuki Gozen|ja|山吹御前}} * {{ill|Orihiromitsu|ja|折井宏光}} - Historical painter who depicted Kanasa Mori-sumi.<ref>[http://suwagunshihaku.org/article/56094157.html 諏訪湖博物館特別展: "Drawing Kanasa Mori-sumi ~ Orihiromitsu Exhibition" (2012)]</ref>
## Notes

{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎|12=draft|12=draft}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|12=draft|12=draft}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝|12=draft|12=draft}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|12=draft|12=draft}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛|12=draft|12=draft}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲|12=draft|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱|12=draft|12=draft}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事|12=draft|12=draft}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家|12=draft|12=draft}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|12=draft|12=draft}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術|12=draft|12=draft}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮|12=draft|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会|12=draft|12=draft}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬|12=draft|12=draft}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|12=draft|12=draft}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国|12=draft|12=draft}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町|12=draft|12=draft}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft|12=draft}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>
## References * ''Nagano Encyclopedia'' (長野県百科事典), published by {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞|12=draft|12=draft}}. * ''Dictionary of Medieval Japanese History'' (日本中世史事典), published by {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店|12=draft|12=draft}}.
## See also * {{ill|Suwa Shinbō|ja|諏訪神党|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yamabuki Gozen|ja|山吹御前|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Orihiromitsu|ja|折井宏光|12=draft|12=draft}} - Historical painter who depicted Kanasa Mori-sumi.<ref>[http://suwagunshihaku.org/article/56094157.html 諏訪湖博物館特別展: "Drawing Kanasa Mori-sumi ~ Orihiromitsu Exhibition" (2012)]</ref>

revision.text:
{{Infobox person | image = Suwa Morizumi.jpg | caption = Kanasa Mori-sumi (諏訪盛澄 / 金刺盛澄), depicted in the Edo-period work ''Zenkō Kojitsu'' by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|ja|菊池容斎|12=draft|12=draft}}. | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | occupation = Priest of {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|12=draft|12=draft}} Lower Shrine, Samurai }}
'''Kanasa Mori-sumi''' (金刺 盛澄, also known as Suwa Mori-sumi) was a priest ({{ill|Ōhō|ja|大祝|12=draft|12=draft}}) and samurai of the {{ill|Suwa Taisha|ja|諏訪大社|1=Suwa-taisha|12=draft|12=draft}} Lower Shrine during the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1000/100115.htm 諏訪市博物館 - ''Suwa City Museum: Kanasa Mori-sumi'']</ref> He is also referred to as {{ill|Suwa Mori-sumi|ja|諏訪盛澄|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/gojuuon/13_su/~su_69.htm ''Suwa City Museum: Suwa Mori-sumi'']</ref> His younger brother was {{ill|Tezuka Mitsumori|ja|手塚光盛|12=draft|12=draft}}.
## Biography Initially, Kanasa Mori-sumi joined {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshinaka|ja|源義仲|12=draft|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Genpei War|ja|治承・寿永の乱|12=draft|12=draft}} but later returned to his homeland to oversee the {{ill|Misayama Ritual|ja|御射山神事|12=draft|12=draft}} and left his younger brother Mitsumori in his stead. Due to his longstanding affiliation with the {{ill|Heike|ja|平家|12=draft|12=draft}}, recorded in the ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}'' under the entry for {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (1187), August 15, Mori-sumi was captured by {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|12=draft|12=draft}} and placed under the custody of {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Yoritomo initially intended to execute Mori-sumi. However, Kagetoki, recognizing Mori-sumi's skill in {{ill|archery|ja|弓術|12=draft|12=draft}}, persuaded Yoritomo to allow Mori-sumi to demonstrate his abilities before his execution. At the {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮|12=draft|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Hōjōe|ja|放生会|12=draft|12=draft}}, Mori-sumi performed an exceptional feat of {{ill|yabusame|ja|流鏑馬|12=draft|12=draft}}, accurately shooting eight targets, their fragments, and the stands holding them. Impressed, Yoritomo pardoned him. Following Kagetoki's further petitions, several of Yoshinaka's retainers were also spared and became {{ill|gokenin|ja|御家人|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%8F%E8%A8%AA%E7%9B%9B%E6%BE%84-1084644 ''Suwa Mori-sumi''] - Kotobank</ref>
In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|12=draft|12=draft}} 4 (1193), Mori-sumi accompanied Yoritomo on hunts in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国|12=draft|12=draft}}, where he continued to serve at ceremonial events involving archery. By 1203, his activities are still noted in historical records. In modern-day {{ill|Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町|12=draft|12=draft}}, a mound called "Kajiwara Zuka" commemorates Mori-sumi's gratitude to Kagetoki.
## Gallery <gallery widths="200px" heights="140px"> 梶原塚（下諏訪町）.jpg|Kajiwara Zuka (Shimosuwa Town), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft|12=draft}} 梶原塚全景.jpg|View of Kajiwara Zuka near Suwa Taisha Shimosha Akimiya </gallery>
## References * ''Nagano Encyclopedia'' (長野県百科事典), published by {{ill|Shinano Mainichi Shimbun|ja|信濃毎日新聞|12=draft|12=draft}}. * ''Dictionary of Medieval Japanese History'' (日本中世史事典), published by {{ill|Asakura Shoten|ja|朝倉書店|12=draft|12=draft}}.
## See also * {{ill|Suwa Shinbō|ja|諏訪神党|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Suwa Clan|ja|諏訪氏|1=Suwa clan|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yamabuki Gozen|ja|山吹御前|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Orihiromitsu|ja|折井宏光|12=draft|12=draft}} - Historical painter who depicted Kanasa Mori-sumi.<ref>[http://suwagunshihaku.org/article/56094157.html 諏訪湖博物館特別展: "Drawing Kanasa Mori-sumi ~ Orihiromitsu Exhibition" (2012)]</ref>
